mnemonics.title: BASE CASE REMEMBER
mnemonics.content: ALWAYS THINK: BASE CONDITION STOPS INFINITE RECURSION - WITHOUT IT, STACK OVERFLOW WILL OCCUR

mnemonics.title: RECURSION STEPS
mnemonics.content: THINK: RECURSIVE CASE MOVES TOWARD BASE CASE - EACH CALL SIMPLIFIES THE PROBLEM

mnemonics.title: FIBONACCI MEMOIZATION
mnemonics.content: STORE AND REUSE: CALCULATE ONCE, LOOKUP MANY TIMES - CONVERT EXPONENTIAL TO LINEAR

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity of a problem of size n
acronyms.usage: Used to analyze how runtime grows with input size in recursive algorithms
Represents algorithms that split problem in half each step, like binary search

acronyms.expansion: Minimum moves for Tower of Hanoi with n disks
acronyms.usage: Formula to calculate total moves needed: 2^n - 1

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a STACK OF PLATES where each recursive call adds a plate on top. The base case is when all plates are removed. Each plate contains local variables and return address.
Draw an INVERTED TREE where the root is the initial call, branches are recursive calls. For Fibonacci, the tree branches become exponentially larger as depth increases.
Think of it as a CONVEYOR BELT where the last thing done is calling itself. The compiler can 'reboot' the same stack frame instead of building a new tower.
Think of it as the GROUND FLOOR of a building. Each recursive call goes down floors until reaching ground (base case), then the answers come back up.

analogies.analogy: Like looking into two mirrors facing each other - the image reflects infinitely, but each reflection is a 'smaller' version of the original until it fades out (base case).
Like building a tower from top to bottom. You start with the top block (n), then add the one below (n-1), and so on until you reach the foundation (1).
Like finding a word in a dictionary by opening to the middle, deciding which half contains the word, then repeating on that half - each step cuts search space in half.
Like learning to dance in a line - you master the simplest move (1 disk), then build on it for 2 disks, then 3, etc. Each level adds complexity to what you already know.
